summary:
Benign intracranial hypertension typically presents with headache, visual changes such as papilledema; proptosis does not feature prominently among its characteristic signs. To answer "All are features" questions accurately requires recognizing which symptom deviates from these established patterns. In this case, proptosis diverges significantly enough to be considered an exception based primarily upon differential diagnosis considerations involving distinct pathologies affecting eye positioning independently of increased cerebrospinal fluid pressure. Agent2 concludes by emphasizing how identifying unique presenting factors helps distinguish between conditions sharing similar underlying mechanisms yet manifesting differently clinically. ```plaintext
Benign intracranial hypotension usually manifests with headaches and vision problems like papilledema, while proptosis is rarely associated with this condition. Thus, proptosis distinguishes itself as non-characteristic for benign intracranial hypertensive patients.
```
